name: Pulmonary tuberculosis
presentation: Chronic productive cough >3 weeks, often with haemoptysis
Systemic B symptoms: drenching night sweats, fever, weight loss, fatigue
Risk factors: TB contact, immunosuppression (HIV, steroids), origin from high-prevalence country, homelessness, prison
May be incidental finding on CXR in asymptomatic patient — screen contacts
signs: Often no specific signs in early disease
Cachexia and lymphadenopathy in advanced or disseminated disease
Apical crackles or bronchial breathing — cavitating upper lobe disease
Signs of extrapulmonary TB: lymphadenopathy, spinal tenderness (Pott's disease), meningism

bedside: Sputum microscopy: acid-fast bacilli (ZN stain) — 3 early morning samples; positive confirms active TB
Sputum culture (gold standard): Mycobacterium tuberculosis — takes 2–6 weeks
Tuberculin skin test (Mantoux) — positive suggests exposure/infection; false negative if immunosuppressed
bloods: IGRA (interferon-gamma release assay — e.g. QuantiFERON): preferred over Mantoux for latent TB screening; not affected by BCG
HIV test: all patients with TB must be offered HIV testing
LFTs/FBC: baseline before starting hepatotoxic drugs (rifampicin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide)
imaging: CXR: upper lobe consolidation/cavitation, fibrosis, calcified Ghon focus — apical shadowing is classic
CT chest: confirms extent, detects miliary pattern (1–2 mm nodules throughout both lungs)
CT/MRI spine if Pott's disease suspected: vertebral destruction, paraspinal abscess

mx: Initial phase 2 months: RIPE — Rifampicin + Isoniazid + Pyrazinamide + Ethambutol (4-drug regimen; ethambutol added routinely since 2006 NICE guidelines)
Continuation phase 4 months: Rifampicin + Isoniazid only (2 drugs, total 6 months treatment)
Latent TB: 3 months isoniazid + rifampicin OR 6 months isoniazid alone (both with pyridoxine)
traps: Drug side effects — must know all four: Rifampicin = orange secretions + potent enzyme INDUCER (affects OCP, warfarin, antiretrovirals); Isoniazid = peripheral neuropathy (prevent with pyridoxine/B6) + enzyme INHIBITOR; Pyrazinamide = hyperuricaemia/gout; Ethambutol = optic neuritis (check visual acuity before and during)
Rifampicin is an enzyme INDUCER, isoniazid is an enzyme INHIBITOR — opposite effects; rifampicin reduces efficacy of OCP (use alternative contraception)
TB meningitis: treat for minimum 12 months (not 6), add dexamethasone
Directly observed therapy (DOT) indicated for: homeless, poor concordance, prisoners
Notify Public Health England (notifiable disease) — all active TB cases must be reported; contact tracing required
